Based on the intent of this PR, I suggest that we add instructions on how users can build, include, and call the plugin API in their own plugin, rather than introducing `develop.sh`.

What I like about this PR:
- The TensorBoard team has 1 bazel command to manually test it.
- Brings awareness to setOnReload and how to call it.

I think the main benefit of having an example_raw_scalars plugin is to provide some template that users can copy. If users run the example and see `setOnReload` working, I think the natural followup one asks is "let me try using it!". At that point, they are stuck because it is unclear how './lib' is built and included.

It would also be nice for users to be able to use the plugin lib without having to understand Bazel, genrules, where "org_tensorflow_tensorboard" comes from, "tf_js_binary", etc. The necessary concepts to use the plugin API are really:

- The entry point is `components/experimental/plugin_lib/index.ts`
- It needs to be bundled with one's favorite bundler (e.g. rollup, webpack, parcel, esbuild)
- API methods are namespaced, e.g. `setOnReload` is exposed on `lib.core.setOnReload`

I think for now, it might be best to just add documentation in the README explaining those 3 points, or even checking in a pre-built .js bundle for users.  Wdyt?

My main feedback here is that `register_definitions` seems fairly magical. As we briefly discussed, I think it would be easy for a user to guess that it writes the definitions to some sort of cloud service.

IMO the least magical thing would be to communicate to the user that they're setting a module-scoped variable.  Either with a name like `assign_definitions_to_module`. Or a reserved attr called something like `defs` and then they do
```
defs = definitions(...)
```

Are those directions that you considered and rejected? What's the downside?

Sorry, the whole code is broken anyway, the typo is just a tiny part.  Let me explain:

`pkg_resources.get_distribution` does work on Python package names, not ob dotted paths. While in most cases the dotted path of the root of the package is the same, this is not always the case.
Anyway, here it does not matter, because it tries to gfetch a dotted path inside the package, which wont work. See also
https://setuptools.pypa.io/en/latest/pkg_resources.html#distribution-objects

With or without typo: `DEXTERITY_WITH_CONSTRAINS` is always `False`.

This LGTM - but I have a few thoughts. I found this in my "Review Requested" lol from a long time ago but gave it another review as if it were fresh :-D 

I don't really see a need for slots here. Slots seem most useful when rendering another component or other HTML within another component. To use them to pass a string around seems off. The places where you use slots could be done more cleanly IMO using a computed property.

Perhaps we can remove the `type` on the prop - but instead use a custom validator that checks if the input is a string, prints a warning if it isn't that says "we're putting KEmtpyPlaceholder in this component where you gave us not a string" but then we can accept `null` or `undefined` values as well.

Anyway - great work - happy to chat more about it synchronously as well.

Previously, mhaoli (Minghao Li) wrote…
The implementation of original snippet_client uses log.exception, but I found it is a problem of python2, not python3. According to internal post "Python tips 046", re-raising in python3 does preserve trace. I have did experiments to verify the conclusion. So if we use `log.exception` here, we will print the error trace twice. Also, I found in experiments that the error trace printed by log.exception is incomplete when the error occurs in a context manager. While the trace printed by re-raising is complete. So in my opinion the following code is the best practice of re-raising, does that make sense? ``` except Exception as e: log.error(....) raise ```
Why do we need to log anything at all in the `except` block if the stacktrace is preserved? Note, `except ... as e: raise` is different from `except ... as e: raise e` So are you proposing we do the `raise` without `e`, which differs from your current code and inline comment ___ *[mobly/snippet/client_base.py, line 235 at r1](https://reviewable.io/reviews/google/mobly/795#-Mwd_58k9qDiSxg8WorX:-Mwfmjct6SPA33kvmdsT:b-fsks9g) ([raw file](https://github.com/google/mobly/blob/6140b5a29d4e6c4f4d666e9c38b716fed01d058c/mobly/snippet/client_base.py#L235)):*
Previously, mhaoli (Minghao Li) wrote…
I'm not sure about the "default logic". I marked those functions to be private just because I want to expose as few public methods as possible, so we have the right to modify them if needed. Do you think it's better to follow the "default logic"?
In Java, you simply cannot override private methods, which is a hint of the OOP philosophy of private methods. So if we expect a method to be overridden, we should probably not make it private.

This looks mostly good, and seems to be working as intended. Here are some thoughts:

- We should not remove the `schemas-juju-latest.json` link and make sure it points to the current latest which is `schemas-juju-3.0.3.json`, otherwise when we have a new schema, the `facade.py` [will pick the fir](https://github.com/juju/python-libjuju/blob/fd89d1a0bb4f162969b3c479cb4701c501cc6786/juju/client/facade.py#L940)st schema in the directory that matches with `re.compile(r'[0-9]+\.[0-9-]+[\.\-][0-9a-z]+(\.[0-9]+)?')` 

- We should give a good error message if 2.9 client is being tried with this, instead of the weird `AttributeError: 'CharmOrigin' object has no attribute 'os'` error which is a random error that looks like a bug from the user's perspective.

I think there are a couple things we can do to address this SSH issue, one short-term and one long-term.

For an immediate workaround, we can allow the user to specify the `nics` and avoid using SSH to find the common interface.  This should already be handled by `get_common_interfaces` [here](https://github.com/horovod/horovod/blob/master/horovod/runner/driver/driver_service.py#L214), just need to test that it works.

The more general solution is to find the common interfaces without requiring passwordless SSH.  This should also be doable, but will require more work.  Finding the common interfaces essentially consists of three parts:

1. Launch task servers on every worker host as shown [here](https://github.com/horovod/horovod/blob/master/horovod/runner/driver/driver_service.py#L49).
2. Have every worker probe the next worker using its set of interfaces to determine which are "routable". This is shown [here](https://github.com/horovod/horovod/blob/master/horovod/runner/common/util/network.py#L193). This is necessary because some interfaces will show up on all hosts but will not actually be capable of routing traffic on the network (e.g., `lo`).
3. Intersect the routable interfaces from each worker to find a common set, then we can choose any among these and be sure it will work.

My thinking is that we should be able to use the existing `get_common_interfaces` code, but swap out the part where we instantiate the task servers (see [here](https://github.com/horovod/horovod/blob/master/horovod/runner/driver/driver_service.py#L105)). Instead of using SSH, we can use Ray primitives to create the servers via dependency injection, or via environment similar to how we handle Kubeflow in `get_remote_command`.  

What do you think?

Please also look at the Sourcery refactoring and incorporate its requests (if you feel they're spurious or unnecessary, let's talk).

Also, this PR needs to be linked to https://github.com/bachya/simplisafe-python/issues/85 (assuming it either (a) fixes it or (b) makes progress against it).

I probably need to close #890 and create a more approachable summary after the discussion on #915, but I just didn't get to it yet.
***
From #915:

> Cross-staff music: PartStaffExporter currently bumps all voice numbers in the subsequent part staff to ensure distinct voice numbers, i.e. to ensure the 99% use case of no cross staff. When we WANT cross-staff, PartStaffExporter needs to get that knowledge from somewhere. Something like, per measure, if two music21 voices share the same .id/.group and .retain = True, if they've not already been given the same .sequence, give them the same .sequence and renumber everything else around it. This is a bit like the implementation in flattenUnnecessaryVoices, above, but different insofar as it would need to be calculated per measure over all voices from all PartStaffs in a joinable group.

Nasty problem. See also https://github.com/w3c/mnx/issues/183.

FWIW, in typeshed these are typed as `Optional[bool]`. This is really context-dependent: if one of these is typed as returning `None` or `Literal[False]`, then mypy at least can infer some special stuff about the context managers as it knows they won't swallow errors. (refs: they reverted https://github.com/python/mypy/issues/7577 but reachability is changed by this: https://github.com/python/mypy/issues/7666)

b) the json_response is stored as an instance attribute of the FMC class that can be inspected in the situation a None is returned from a send_to_api call.

The name and location of this handler rub me the wrong way, since we'd shoehorn this into the `conda` namespace, making it "private" using the leading underscore *and* overwrite the base class with a dynamically created subclass.

I think that's far too exciting for this functionality, it should not get in the way and not risk the runtime issues of the decorated code. It's as if we're telling people importing conda (e.g. 3rd party developers) that they shouldn't use it, but it's still there, just for the conda code itself and nothing else.

I would suggest doing the following:

- move this into a new module `conda/deprecations.py` (analog to `conda/exceptions.py`)
- remove the leading underscore to enable other conda libraries to depend on it to deprecate things
- rewrite it using `wrapt` since [it's the most correct solution](https://hynek.me/articles/decorators/) for writing decorators and we need to be correct (`functools.wraps` isn't enough)

We _could_ release the result as an actual package on PyPI (but then why not use https://github.com/briancurtin/deprecation?)

I think you have done a great deal of work (creating module files, parsing them to internal representation, module-pool, ...) :+1: , however we still need to adjust some things.

My advice would be:
1) Start with the python preprocessor. Think about the representation of the modules in C++. **Resulting files should not be a valid JSON, they should be a valid C++ (that also encodes the content of JSON)**. I have left a comment in the relevant part describing how you can approach this.
2) Create some code that can convert the C++ module representation that you have created in the step before to a representation that is accepted by the JSON library that you added.
3) When you initialize module-pool you should not interact with the filesystem. Work only with the C++ files (**don't read them from the disk, let them be included during the compilation using what is already in the language `#include`**) that you generated from json modules.

I think these are the most important things to get right, after they are resolved we can move to a another iteration with the review.

This PR introduces a variety of different, distinct changes, that I have comments on individually. As such, I figured it might be better to write them here, rather than at specific lines.

### Increasing "filetype" intelligence

All for this. Yay! I was originally worried about the compatibility with the config transformer, but it looks like the config transformer just attempts all files anyway, so we should be fine.

### Keyword Detector Improvements

TBH, this file continues to mutate, and becomes increasingly hard to understand -- so it takes me a while to understand your changes. I like your `FOLLOWED_BY_COMPARATION_QUOTES_REQUIRED_REGEX` and `FOLLOWED_BY_REV_COMPARATION_QUOTES_REQUIRED_REGEX`, but I'm uncertain of the implications of modifying the core `DENYLIST_REGEX` and `SECRET` regexes.

It *should* be the case that secret values don't have quotes in them: but I need to think more deeply about whether we can assert this.

### KeywordXML

Ok, this absolutely needs to change. Along the same lines of why the KeywordDetector is in need for some serious love (to increase code flexibility), I'm against a direct copy-paste of the `KeywordDetector` to make a version for XML files.

Have you considered writing an XML [transformer](https://github.com/Yelp/detect-secrets/blob/pre-v1-launch/docs/design.md#transformers) instead? This will allow it to take advantage of all the other features that v1 provides. For prior art, check out [YAMLTransformer](https://github.com/Yelp/detect-secrets/blob/pre-v1-launch/detect_secrets/transformers/yaml.py#L25).

Thanks a lot Philip, LGTM.

I have 2 follow-up questions / comments, which we can address (or not) in other PRs:

- As discussed offline, JIT "isn't supported" for the datapoints on these dispatchers. What we mean by "not supported" is that the datapoints will be treated as a pure Tensors, and the output will be a Tensor too. I assume this can lead to unexpected results and confusion, e.g.:

    ```py
    mask = Mask(...)
    out = T(mask)
    # - unaware user expects a Mask but gets a Tensor
    # - `T` treats mask as an image, not as a mask!
    #    Might result in different behaviour (very bad) or confusing error message?
    ```

    so: **should we fail loudly** with a good error message in the disaptchers when `is_scripting()` is True and `is_simple_tensor()` is False?


- Side note: should we be more lenient with `mypy` checks in the prototype area?
We introduced strict checks in https://github.com/pytorch/vision/pull/4513 (and I approved the PR, so I'll concede I'm sort of rebutting myself here).
But the the prototype area is where we want to be able to move fast, and try potentially crazy things without having to worry too much about type checks / docs / super clean tests. In this specific case, the introduction of `warn_return_any` could have been the reason for these convoluted checks in the first place which complicates the code and slows down iteration speed. Perhaps we may want to relax the mypy requirements in order to experiment faster in the prototype area?

Thank you for this contribution!

I am not entirely sure what you want to achieve when using artifacts. If I understand correctly, you use artifacts as a way to store the result of the build, with a key based on the package version listed in the `setup.py` file. However, relying on the version identifier seems very risky: while we are on a working branch, the same version identifier might be used with different dependencies sets.
If I understood your intention correctly, I recommend to instead use the [cache action](https://docs.github.com/en/actions/using-workflows/caching-dependencies-to-speed-up-workflows#using-the-cache-action) with a `hashFiles('setup.py')` key instead. This will save you the hassle of managing directories, as the `cache` action will always load and restore to the same, and will make sure that the cache is invalidated if the _contents_ of `setup.py` change 🙂

While the change looks good in isolation, to get a green CI at https://github.com/pycontribs/ansi2html/actions/runs/4925721788/jobs/8800292224?pr=214#step:8:40 we probably need a dedicated pull request to appease flake8 first, and then rebase onto latest `main` after merge.

> Agree that this is weird, but I think that this weirdness is just inherited from the weirdness of @logger. In a world where there were only handlers, the two responsibilities would be very distinct: DagsterLogManager is a log that produces messages with Dagster metadata and has a set of handlers on it. DagsterLogConversionHandler is a special handler that sneakily converts normal python logs into Dagster-formatted logs, and then calls all of DagsterLogManager's handlers with this new record.

Just spitballin' here: what if we relied on the Python logging framework for dispatching instead of implementing on our own in `DagsterLogManager`.  E.g.  `context.log` would point to an object produced by invoking `logging.getLogger("__dagster__").  Then, for each Dagster `LoggerDefinition`, we'd create a logging `Handler` that wraps the `Logger` returned by the `LoggerDefinition`, and have that `Handler` subscribe to the "__dagster__" logger?
